tools_analyzed.name: Cursor IDE (Cursor 3.0)
innovations: Agents Window — agent-first interface built from scratch, not VS Code fork. Primary workspace is agent management, not file editing. Launch via Cmd+Shift+P → Agents Window.
Multi-agent parallelism — run agents across repos, worktrees, cloud, SSH simultaneously. Tiled Layout for managing many agent panes at once.
Worktree isolation — /worktree command creates isolated git worktrees so agents operate on their own branch without touching main, eliminating file collision.
Local↔cloud handoff — move a running agent session from local to cloud (keeps working while you close laptop) or cloud to local (for hands-on editing).
/best-of-n — run the same prompt across multiple models in parallel, Conductor agent compares outputs. GPT vs Claude side-by-side in isolated worktrees.
Design Mode — annotate UI elements directly in embedded browser. Shift+drag to select, Cmd+L to add to chat. Multimodal frontend feedback.
Plugin marketplace — 30+ MCP plugins (Datadog, Linear, Slack, Figma, PlanetScale). Private team marketplaces for internal plugins.
Auto-review Run Mode (Cursor 3.6) — agents self-review their own PRs before human review.
tools_analyzed.gap: No visual agent thinking timeline. Agent actions shown as chat messages only — no real-time visualization of what the agent is doing, which files it's reading, what decisions it's making. The 'glass' UI has layout regressions on non-16:9 monitors (ultrawide, vertical). Cloud agents always run in Max Mode (expensive). No ambient/proactive intelligence — all interactions are user-initiated.
tools_analyzed.opencode_opportunity: OpenCode already has session viz (activity feed + codebase map). Leapfrog: build a real-time agent thinking display that shows tool calls as live cards, codebase navigation as a heatmap overlay, and decision points as branch nodes — not just a chat log. Add proactive agents that suggest work without being asked.

tools_analyzed.name: GitHub Copilot / Copilot Chat
innovations: Agent Skills — SKILL.md folders auto-detected from .github/skills, loaded on-demand. Teach agent domain knowledge without prompt engineering.
Background agents — local agent sessions continue running when closed. Parallel + long-running tasks natively. Handoff to cloud agents via 'Continue in'.
Agent hooks — run custom shell commands at agent lifecycle points (PreToolUse, PostToolUse, SessionStart, Stop). Compatible with Claude Code hook format.
Plan agent — structured 4-phase workflow (Discovery → Alignment → Design → Refinement). Invoked via /plan. Clarifying questions before writing code.
Subagents with constrained tool access — define subagents that only have read/search/web tools. Safety without sacrificing capability.
Message steering & queuing — send follow-up messages while agent is working. Drag-and-drop reorder queued messages. Mid-task steering.
Inline agent mode — invoke agent capabilities directly from inline chat (Shift+Cmd+I) without switching panels.
Unified sessions view — all agent sessions (local, cloud, CLI) in one panel with live status, progress, file change stats.
Claude extended thinking display — collapsible reasoning section showing Claude's chain-of-thought.
tools_analyzed.gap: Chat is still the primary modality. No spatial or visual intelligence display. Agent thinking is text-only (even the 'extended thinking' is just collapsible text). Customization requires .md files in specific directories — no in-app management UI. Cloud agents only work with pull request workflow (until April 2026 change).
tools_analyzed.opencode_opportunity: OpenCode has a richer UI canvas (SolidJS, not VS Code extension constraints). Build visual agent orchestration that Copilot can't: drag-and-drop agent workflow composition, real-time DAG visualization of agent steps, side-by-side agent output comparison without needing /best-of-n.

tools_analyzed.name: Raycast
innovations: AI Extensions — turn natural language into OS actions. Tools + Instructions + Evals framework. Extensions can confirm before executing (human-in-the-loop). Private team extensions.
Quick AI + AI Chat unified — single composer powers both one-off questions and long conversations. Context persists across sessions.
Window Management API — programmatic control of window positions, sizes, desktops. Grid overlay for visual positioning. Layout presets.
Global hotkey (Alt+Space) — instant summon over any app. Feels like OS-integrated, not a separate app.
New architecture (v2) — hybrid native/web stack. Swift/AppKit shell + WebKit WebView + Node backend. Cross-platform (macOS + Windows) from one codebase.
Proactive intelligence via AI Extensions — watch clipboard, detect URLs, suggest actions. Ambient without being intrusive.
Translucent windows, native tooltips over WebView content — eliminates 'web app' feel.
tools_analyzed.gap: No code editing or agent loop. Raycast is a launcher/productivity tool, not a development environment. AI is query/response — no autonomous agents that persist. Skills are context-only, not executable workflows. No multi-agent orchestration.
tools_analyzed.opencode_opportunity: Steal Raycast's window management and global hotkey paradigm. OpenCode already runs as a desktop app (Electron on desktop, but there's also the browser/web app). Add Alt+Space instant overlay for quick agent queries without context switch. Bring Raycast's AI Extension model to development: natural language 'open that file' / 'run the tests' / 'deploy this' as first-class agent actions.

tools_analyzed.name: Linear
innovations: Keyboard-first, mouse-friendly — every action has a mnemonic keyboard shortcut (S for Status, P for Priority, A for Assign). Command palette (Cmd+K) is universal entry point with fuzzy search. Progressive learning: beginners use mouse, see shortcuts in menus, graduate to keyboard.
Command palette as universal action hub — search issues, projects, commands all in one fuzzy search dialog. No separate menus for different actions.

Opinionated design system (Linear Method) — publicly documented product philosophy. Dark-first design. Single lavender accent (#5e6ad2). 4-step surface ladder replaces shadows. Aggressive negative tracking on display typography.
Dev toolbar with live feature flags — toggle new UI on/off instantly. Built-in color picker tool (built with Claude Code) for rapid design token iteration.
Contextual menus with safe-area triangles — sub-menus stay open by drawing a geometric safe zone between cursor and menu. Eliminates the 'upside-down L' mouse path.
Optimistic UI — update state locally first, sync in background. Errors only surface when they actually happen.
6 microstates per interactive element — default, hover, focus (keyboard), active (pressed), disabled, loading. Every state designed, not defaulted.
tools_analyzed.gap: No AI-native surface at all. Linear has an API for agents but the UI itself is traditional CRUD. No agent chat, no AI commands, no proactive intelligence. The command palette is for human commands, not agent delegation.
tools_analyzed.opencode_opportunity: Apply Linear's craft philosophy to OpenCode's AI surfaces. The command palette (Cmd+K) should be THE universal entry point: 'fix this bug', 'write tests', 'explain this code', 'deploy'. Every agent interaction should have a keyboard shortcut. Apply Linear's opinionated constraints: one accent color, deliberate motion curves, interaction completeness on every element. The 'premium UI' playbook.

tools_analyzed.name: Arc Browser
innovations: Vertical sidebar replaces horizontal tabs — uses abundant vertical space on modern monitors. Tab titles fully readable. Pinned sections + folders.
Spaces — per-context browsing areas with separate pinned tabs, themes, gradients, and isolated login sessions. Instant switch with Cmd+1/2/3. Work vs Personal never mixes.
Command Bar (Cmd+T) — universal search across tabs, history, bookmarks, commands. One entry point replaces toolbar + bookmarks bar + URL bar.
Little Arc — minimal floating browser window for quick tasks. No chrome, one tab, 36px header. Match interface weight to task weight.
Peek — opens links as preview overlay instead of new tab. If relevant, pin it; if not, close it. Zero tab explosion.
Auto-archive — automatically tucks away untouched tabs. Users typically maintain single-digit active tabs.
Boosts — user CSS/JS customizations per site. Fix annoying websites without waiting for the vendor.
tools_analyzed.gap: Arc stopped major development in late 2025 (team shifted to Dia browser). Memory usage issues. No AI integration at all (ironically for a browser that rethought UX). No agent capabilities.
tools_analyzed.opencode_opportunity: Arc's Spaces concept for agent workspaces: each project gets its own 'Space' with pinned files, recent context, active agents, and separate git state. Vertical sidebar for agent sessions (OpenCode already has session tabs in a horizontal bar — vertical would scale better). Little Arc as 'Little Agent' — a minimal floating window for quick agent queries without opening the full app.

tools_analyzed.name: Experimental AI-Native Desktop OS Projects

projects.name: Yantrik OS
projects.innovation: AI is the shell, not an add-on. Single Rust binary with Slint UI. 4-stage proactive pipeline (Detect→Generate→Score→Deliver). 9+ instincts (email watch, routine learning, commitment tracking). Bond system (Stranger→Partner). Local-first with Qwen 3.5 4B offline. 116+ tools.
projects.key_pattern: Proactive agent pipeline with scoring — the AI decides when to speak based on relevance scoring, not user initiation.

projects.name: SynthesisOS
projects.innovation: Your requests become syscalls dispatched by a Rust kernel. 60+ native macOS tools. Tool RAG (semantic search over tool definitions). A2UI protocol — agents return structured UI components, not text. 3D spatial UI with Three.js backdrop. Jarvis mode (desktop takeover). Dual mode: Zen (minimal) / God (raw JSON + logs).
projects.key_pattern: A2UI protocol — agents return structured UI blocks (tables, galleries, maps) instead of markdown. 'Food mode' for debugging — flip any card to see raw agent decisions.

projects.name: SomaOS
projects.innovation: Both human and AI are first-class users of the same desktop. Custom DRM/KMS compositor. Dual-interface native apps — same data model, two interfaces (GUI for human, AgentAPI for AI). Agent mode: AI takes full control of desktop. Human watches, can interrupt via HITL gate.
projects.key_pattern: Co-inhabitant model — agent is not a tool but a peer. Structured perception via AgentAPI instead of screen-scraping. HITL as OS-level primitive.

projects.name: PrismOS
projects.innovation: 8 collaborative agents with LangGraph debate pipeline. 7-dimensional Spectrum Graph for persistent memory. Ctrl+Space global hotkey. Cognitive Imprint (adaptive 5-axis personality). WASM-isolated sandbox. 15 curated models with hardware-aware recommendations. Zero cloud — everything local via Ollama.
projects.key_pattern: Multi-agent debate for consensus before action. Spectrum Graph for cross-dimensional memory tracking. Cognitive Imprint for personalized interaction style.

projects.name: DeskTalk
projects.innovation: Generative apps — tell the AI what you need and it creates a LiveApp (self-contained HTML app) on your desktop. Persists across sessions. Edit in-place via conversation. Browser-based OS environment.
projects.key_pattern: LiveApp generation — AI creates interactive tools on demand rather than you installing software. Conversational app creation.

projects.name: Athen
projects.innovation: Single native Rust binary (~30MB) with Tauri 2. Senses (email, calendar, Telegram, generic input). Wake-ups / proactive scheduling with autonomy-band controls. Risk system with regex + LLM fallback. MCP runtime. Bring your own LLM or run local (Ollama/llama.cpp). Idles in tray with negligible CPU.
projects.key_pattern: Always-on tray agent with proactive scheduling. Risk-scored autonomous actions. Bring-your-own-provider philosophy.

tools_analyzed.gap: All are alpha/pre-alpha quality. None have production UX polish. Yantrik requires Alpine Linux VM. SynthesisOS only works on macOS + needs API keys. SomaOS requires a custom bootable image. PrismOS needs Ollama + model downloads. None are ready for mainstream developer audiences. None have the code editing capability OpenCode has.
tools_analyzed.opencode_opportunity: OpenCode is uniquely positioned: already a production-quality desktop app with browser TUI + Electron + core agent engine. Add proactive agent instincts (like Yantrik's watch instincts) that wake up in the background during idle. Let agents spawn 'mini apps' (DeskTalk-style) for temporary tools. Add a 'God mode' flip (SynthesisOS) to see every agent decision, tool call, and token. Build A2UI-style structured agent responses before the standard exists.

white_space: Visual agent thinking timeline — No tool (not Cursor, not Copilot, not Claude Code) shows a real-time DAG/flowchart of what the agent is doing. Cursor has a chat log. Copilot shows text. Claude Code is terminal-only. The activity feed in OpenCode's session viz is a start but could become a full interactive decision tree showing tool calls, file reads, reasoning branches, and decision points in real time.

Proactive ambient agent — Every tool is reactive: user types, agent responds. Yantrik OS has the closest pattern with its 4-stage pipeline, but it's not a development tool. An agent that notices 'you've been debugging this test for 20 minutes, here's what I found' or 'your CI is failing, I fixed it' — ambient intelligence in a development context is unexplored territory.

Structured agent response protocol (A2UI) — Agents currently return markdown. SynthesisOS is pioneering agents returning structured UI components. OpenCode could build this: agents return typed blocks (diff_view, file_tree, search_results, graph, table) that the UI renders natively. Makes agent output glanceable and interactive, not a wall of text.

Spaces for agent workspaces — Arc's Spaces concept applied to development: each project/feature/task gets its own Space with pinned files, open sessions, running agents, git state, and terminal history. Switch contexts instantly without losing state.

Agent-as-OS integration — None of the coding tools integrate with the OS beyond file editing. OpenCode's desktop app could add: app launching, window management, notification responses, clipboard monitoring, calendar awareness. The agent operates 'over' the OS, not just inside the editor.

LiveApp generation for dev tools — Tell the agent 'build me a visual diff dashboard for this PR' and it generates a temporary tool. DeskTalk shows this pattern. For developers, this means one-shot utilities: test coverage visualizer, dependency graph explorer, migration progress tracker — all created conversationally, all disposable.

recommended_focus.why: OpenCode already has the session viz infrastructure (activity feed + codebase map + coordination events). No competitor has a real-time, visual agent thinking display. This is a leapfrog opportunity.
recommended_focus.suggestion: Build a live DAG view showing agent tool calls as interactive cards connected by edges. Show which files are being read (heatmap overlay), which commands are running (terminal stream inline), and what decisions the agent made (branch nodes the user can expand). Make the activity feed visually rich — gradient-coded by tool type, with expandable detail panels.

recommended_focus.area: Command Palette as Universal Agent Interface
recommended_focus.why: Linear and Arc proved the command palette is the best power-user pattern. OpenCode already has one. Extend it: Cmd+K becomes the universal entry point for both human commands AND agent delegation. Type 'fix login test' and it routes to the right agent. Type '/explain' and it attaches current selection.
recommended_focus.suggestion: Merge the command palette and agent prompt into one unified interface. Cmd+K shows: recent agent sessions, slash commands, file search, project navigation, AND agent prompts. Fuzzy search across all. Add mode indicators (current agent, model). Show agent suggestions proactively based on what you're doing.

recommended_focus.why: Every coding tool is reactive. The next leap is an agent that notices patterns and offers help without being asked. Yantrik OS, Athen, and PrismOS all have proactive patterns but none in a development context.
recommended_focus.suggestion: Implement 'watch instincts' that run during agent idle time: (1) test failure watcher — notices when tests start failing and investigates, (2) dependency drift detector — notices when package.json changes and suggests updates, (3) PR readiness checker — watches your branch and offers to open a PR when tests pass. Surface these as non-intrusive status indicators, not popups.

what_opencode_already_has: Session visualization with activity feed and codebase map — already built, unique among coding agents
Multi-agent support with delegation UI (@mentions for subagents) — competitive with Cursor/Copilot
Command palette with fuzzy search — competitive with Linear/Arc pattern
Composer picker (agent + model selection) — competitive with Cursor's model selector
Slash commands and prompt attachments — competitive with Copilot
Message timeline with followup queuing — competitive with Copilot's message steering
Desktop app (Electron) + browser TUI + core SDK — multi-surface
Coordination tracking and path claiming — unique, enables safe multi-agent
Terminal panel, review panel, side panel — rich workspace layout
what_to_build_next: Real-time agent thinking DAG visualizer (leapfrog over Cursor/Copilot)
Unified Cmd+K palette that merges commands + agent prompts + project search
Proactive agent instincts (ambient intelligence during idle)
Spaces/workspaces for project context isolation (Arc pattern)
Structured agent response protocol (A2UI-style typed blocks instead of markdown)
Global hotkey overlay for quick agent queries (Raycast Alt+Space pattern)
LiveApp generation for one-shot dev tools (DeskTalk pattern)
Agent-to-agent visual workflow builder (drag-and-drop agent pipelines)
